Abstract.score_string:
Abstract

Saturn: Zero-Configuration AI Service Discovery
Joseph Perrello

AI tools gate access behind API keys. Each application needs its own key, its own
endpoint, its own setup—three tools, three accounts. A student without a credit card
gets zero. Saturn sidesteps the whole ritual by broadcasting AI endpoints over mDNS
and DNS-SD, the protocols behind AirPrint and Chromecast, so that joining a WiFi
network is enough to reach a language model.
Saturn beacons register OpenAI-compatible endpoints under saturn. tcp.local.
and publish metadata plus a short-lived credential in DNS TXT records. Keys rotate every five minutes; grab one off the wire and it dies before you leave the building. Clients
discover beacons, rank them by administrator-set priority, and connect—no accounts,
no credential files. One network administrator replaces per-user key management for
every device on the segment. The security exposure is bounded: Saturn broadcasts only
what fits in a DNS TXT record, and ephemeral rotation makes captured keys worthless
within minutes.
We built six implementations in four languages. One runs on a GL.iNet travel router
next to the DHCP daemon—same box, same boot, but now it hands out AI access
alongside IP addresses. mDNS has handled printers since 2002. Saturn puts language
models on the same wire.

ix

Acknowledgments.score_string:
Acknowledgments
Adam Smith let me chase a strange idea—putting AI on mDNS—and then made me
defend every piece of it. Ram Sundara Raman kept asking what happens when someone
on the network lies, which is how Saturn got a real threat model instead of a hand-wave.
Peter Biehl and the graduate division kept the paperwork from burying me.
Saturn reuses mDNS and DNS-SD almost unchanged. Stuart Cheshire and Marc
Krochmal designed those protocols; the IETF DNSSD working group refined them
over two decades. All Saturn contributes is one new service type. The foundation was
already solid.
This project started because my classmates—people in a graduate CS program—
could not use AI tools without API keys they did not have. Not a skill problem. A
plumbing problem. I wanted to fix the plumbing.

x

Introduction.score_string:
Chapter 1
Introduction
Lewis Strauss predicted in 1954 that atomic energy would become “too cheap to meter” [38]. Generative AI arrived with the same rhetoric. ChatGPT Plus: $20/month [32].
Claude Pro: the same [1]. Stack both and a researcher pays $40 before touching a
usage-based tier that runs past $100.
A developer running three tools—chat, code completion, transcription—against one
Ollama backend types the address three times. Change the port; all three break. Syed
et al. [40] formalized the pattern as AI-as-a-Service: REST endpoints gated by per-user
tokens, each obtained and rotated in isolation. The fragmentation underneath—every
person, every app, managing credentials alone—does more damage than the price tag.
Meli et al. [24] scanned GitHub and found API secrets in over 100,000 repositories.
They notified the owners; 81% of the keys stayed live. Their best scanner caught one
in four—three quarters of leaked static keys, the default at every major AI provider, sit
unrevoked.
Socioeconomic status predicts how much value a person extracts from language
technology, even after access is nominally granted [4]—a gap that compounds in classrooms [14] and holds across whole national economies [6]. Costa et al. [11] call the

1

outcome what it is: technical barriers reinforcing the power asymmetries they were
supposed to dismantle. Per-user subscriptions and per-app credential wrangling lock
out the people with the most to gain.

1.1

The Vision: AI as Network Infrastructure

Join a WiFi network, see the printers, print. Apple shipped Bonjour in 2002 and that
became unremarkable. Two protocols do the work: Multicast DNS (mDNS) resolves
names on the local segment without a central server; DNS-based Service Discovery
(DNS-SD) advertises what each device offers. Guttman [16] described the goal—
“direct communications between two or more computing devices via IP” without manual setup. RFC 1122 [5] had articulated the aspiration before any implementation existed: “It would be ideal if a host implementation of the Internet protocol suite could be
entirely self-configuring.”
AI skipped this step. Ask any chat application for a language model and it asks
back: which provider? which key? which endpoint? The information it needs—an
address, a credential, a protocol dialect—printers have advertised over the wire since
Bonjour shipped.
Eduroam provisions WiFi across hundreds of campuses without per-institution accounts. Campus print services discover printers automatically. Saturn does the same
for language models. Join a network; the AI services on it appear.

1.2

Thesis Statement

Generative AI should be provisioned the way printers and speakers already are: at
the network level. Saturn uses mDNS and DNS-SD—protocols already on billions of

2

devices—to broadcast where AI services live and how to authenticate. No new infrastructure. One administrator per network; everyone else does nothing. The trade-off
is visibility: every device on the segment sees service metadata, including ephemeral
credentials that expire within minutes.

1.3

Saturn and This Thesis

Saturn defines a zero-configuration AI service discovery protocol. Servers register
OpenAI-compatible endpoints under saturn. tcp.local.; clients find them without
setup. Beacons broadcast metadata—where AI services live, how to authenticate—but
carry no API traffic. Clients talk to providers directly; a crashed beacon does not interrupt active connections. The TXT record schema encodes everything a client needs—
endpoint URL, API type, authentication credentials, priority—inside standard DNS-SD
records. No out-of-band configuration channel exists.
A Rust implementation cross-compiled to MIPS32 places Saturn on actual network
infrastructure: a $30 GL.iNet travel router that already assigns IP addresses via DHCP
and resolves names via DNS. Saturn runs alongside these services in the router’s process table, managed by the same init system and configured through the same web
interface. The router deployment moves the feasibility argument from developer workstations to the consumer gateways that define home and small-office networks.
Six client integrations across four languages validate the protocol as a generalpurpose discovery surface. The core Python package handles server and client duties. A TypeScript provider plugs into Vercel’s AI SDK. An Open WebUI plugin and
an MCP server feed coding assistants like Cursor and Continue. A Lua-to-Python
bridge runs inside VLC. Each uses a different mDNS library; all consume the same
saturn. tcp.local. records without a bridging layer.

3

The next chapter surveys existing service-discovery protocols and the AI access
landscape. Chapters 3 and 4 specify the protocol and walk through the six implementations.
Claude, Anthropic’s language model, helped build the knowledge graph that organizes this thesis and gave feedback on drafts. We disclose this because Saturn argues
AI should be accessible—hiding our own use of it would contradict the project. Appendix B gives the full account.

4

Background.score_string:
Chapter 2
Background
A laptop and a printer share a switch and cannot see each other. Service discovery—
making devices visible without intervention—has been producing competing protocols
for three decades. None of them were built for AI. This chapter explains why mDNS
and DNS-SD fit Saturn’s requirements better than the alternatives, then surveys the AI
applications Saturn targets.

2.1

Service Discovery on Local Networks

Plug a laptop into a network and the printer three feet away is invisible. Service discovery is the fix: protocols that let devices announce themselves and clients find what
is available.

2.1.1

NetBIOS

Network Basic Input/Output System (NetBIOS) resolved names on Windows local networks before DNS displaced it [15]. macOS and Linux never adopted it, Microsoft
began phasing it out with Windows 10, and the protocol carries no service metadata—

5

it cannot advertise model lists, API types, or capabilities.

2.1.2

Universal Plug and Play

UPnP’s security record is disqualifying: remote code execution, unauthorized port forwarding, and a history of CVEs that stretches back two decades [30]. All three major
OS vendors have been pulling functionality toward mDNS/DNS-SD instead. UPnP
layers SOAP/XML control on top of SSDP—machinery built for controlling devices,
not for advertising metadata.

2.1.3

DLNA

The Digital Living Network Alliance dissolved in 2017 [12]. Before that, DLNA built a
media streaming standard on UPnP so televisions could find content servers. The scope
never extended beyond audio and video.

2.1.4

WS-Discovery

Web Services Dynamic Discovery (WS-Discovery) targets enterprise environments with
managed infrastructure [27]. Windows uses it for printer discovery alongside mDNS.
The SOAP/XML encoding is heavier than Saturn needs and the library ecosystem is
sparse, but the fundamental mismatch is the network assumption: WS-Discovery expects IT departments. Saturn targets places without them.

2.1.5

Why mDNS/DNS-SD

mDNS resolves names without a server. DNS-SD advertises services using standard
DNS record types. Both run on every major OS—under AirPrint, AirPlay, Chromecast,

6

Spotify Connect—on billions of devices. Guttman [16] defined zero-configuration networking as “direct communications between two or more computing devices via IP”
without manual setup. Nothing in production comes closer. Section 2.2 covers both
protocols.

2.1.6

DHCP

DHCP is Saturn’s closest analogy at the network layer [13]. A device broadcasts DISCOVER; a DHCP server replies with an IP address, gateway, DNS server, and lease
duration; the client acknowledges and is online. No manual configuration. Saturn’s
“join the network, get AI” pitch is consciously modeled on DHCP’s “join the network,
get an IP.”
The analogy has limits. DHCP fires once at connection time, assigns one address,
and requires root privileges. Saturn runs continuously in userspace because AI services
appear and disappear—a local Ollama instance spins up, a cloud key rotates, a beacon
goes offline. A network might host a dozen services with different models, priorities,
and credential types. DHCP carries a handful of fields in its options; Saturn carries
model lists, API types, ephemeral keys, and rotation intervals in DNS TXT records.
They complement each other. On Saturn’s GL.iNet router, DHCP assigns IP addresses while Saturn advertises AI endpoints. Same hardware, same boot sequence,
different layers.

7

2.2

mDNS and DNS-SD

2.2.1

Multicast DNS RFC 6762 [9] specifies Multicast DNS (mDNS). Instead of querying a designated resolver, the client multicasts to 224.0.0.251:5353 (ff02::fb on IPv6). Every device on the
link hears it. Whichever device owns the name responds with its IP address. No server,
no authority, no configuration.
Names end in .local—my-server.local, say. The wire format is ordinary DNS:
A, AAAA, SRV, TXT, PTR. Only two things differ from conventional DNS: transport (multicast instead of unicast) and scope (link-local instead of global). Siddiqui
et al. [36] place mDNS alongside link-local addressing [7] and DNS-SD as the three
pillars of zero-configuration networking.

2.2.2

DNS-Based Service Discovery

mDNS resolves names. DNS-based Service Discovery (DNS-SD, RFC 6763 [8]) answers the next question: what services of type X exist on this network?
Three record types chain together. PTR maps a service type— saturn. tcp.local.—
to instance names. SRV gives hostname and port for each instance. TXT carries keyvalue metadata. A client queries PTR, gets instance names back, then resolves SRV
and TXT for each.
DNS-SD rides on mDNS for link-local scope or on unicast DNS for wide-area.
Saturn uses mDNS only, confining discovery to the local segment.

8

2.2.3

TXT Records and Metadata

TXT records carry key-value pairs. The DNS wire format caps each string at 255 bytes;
a record can hold multiple strings, each independently limited.
Saturn uses eight TXT fields: protocol version, deployment type, API type, base
URL, priority, ephemeral key, rotation interval, feature flags. A client that resolves
these knows enough to connect—no follow-up needed. The 255-byte ceiling constrains
field sizes (an ephemeral key gets roughly 240 usable characters after accounting for
the key name and delimiter), but Saturn’s metadata fits.

2.2.4

Implementations: Bonjour and Avahi

Bonjour shipped with macOS in 2002 and powers AirPrint, AirPlay, and AirDrop [3].
Linux distributions run Avahi [33]. Microsoft added a native mDNS responder in Windows 10. Every major desktop and mobile OS already speaks mDNS. An application
that calls into the system’s resolver—any language, any framework—can discover Saturn services without installing anything.

2.2.5

Adoption in New Domains

Printers and file shares came first. Then AirPlay for speakers and televisions, Chromecast for media casting, Spotify Connect for playback devices. Siljanovski et al. [37]
pushed mDNS into IoT sensor networks. VS Code uses it to locate remote debugging
targets—no IP addresses typed [25].
The closest precedent to Saturn: a community pull request adding mDNS advertisement to Ollama, a local AI inference server [29]. Never merged, but the impulse—what
if AI services announced themselves the way printers do?—is Saturn’s starting point.

9

saturn. tcp instead of ipp. tcp. Same mechanism underneath.

2.2.6

Privacy Considerations

mDNS queries and responses reach every device on the link. Kaiser and Waldvogel [19]
showed that a passive observer can enumerate services, track devices, and correlate
metadata with users. Könings et al. [22] found that 59% of device names in the wild
contain real user names—mDNS announcements as PII.
Saturn inherits all of it. Anyone on the segment sees which AI services exist, which
models they serve, and—depending on beacon naming—who operates them. Kaiser
and Waldvogel [20] proposed hash-based instance names to limit observer knowledge
without breaking discovery; Chapter 7 evaluates Saturn against these known weaknesses.

2.3

The AI Service Landscape

Chat interfaces, coding assistants, voice pipelines—every application that consumes AI
text completion repeats the same setup ritual: endpoint, credentials, model.

2.3.1

Hosted Chat Applications Open WebUI [31], LibreChat [23], Jan [17]: three chat interfaces, same backend, three
separate configurations. Each wants a base URL, an API key, a model identifier. Each
stores its own copy. Change the port on the backend and all three break until someone
re-enters the address by hand.

10

2.3.2

Coding Agents and Assistants

Cursor [2] for code completions, Open WebUI for chat—same Ollama backend, configured twice. Add Continue [10] and it becomes three. Each tool keeps its own URL
and API key. None knows the others exist.

2.3.3

Voice Typing and Transcription

Voice-to-text pipelines pair a speech recognizer (Whisper [35]) with a language model
for cleanup. SuperWhisper [39] runs both on-device. Wispr Flow [42] sends transcripts
to a cloud LLM at $8/month. MacWhisper [18] bundles local models. Want local
transcription with cloud cleanup? Two models, two credentials, two failure points.

11

Design.score_string:
Chapter 3
Design
Add a printer to a network and your laptop finds it. Add an AI service and nothing
happens—someone still has to type a URL, paste a key, pick a model, app by app. Saturn registers AI services the same way printers register themselves: mDNS announcement, DNS-SD metadata, zero client configuration.

3.1

Goals

Join a network, get AI. No installation, no accounts, no keys.
The protocol cannot depend on a single vendor. Anthropic could raise prices, OpenRouter could shut down, a university might switch from one provider to another midsemester. Saturn binds to an interface standard—the OpenAI-compatible REST API—
not a company. Any backend behind that API qualifies.
One person sets up services; every device on the segment inherits them. A university
sysadmin, a roommate with a GPU, someone sharing a cloud key for the afternoon—the
effort scales with the number of services, not the number of people.
Captured credentials are the obvious risk. Someone records a key from a beacon and

12

walks out. So cloud-facing beacons rotate keys every five minutes; a stolen credential
dies before you reach a different network.

3.2

Audiences

Configuration labor does not disappear. Saturn concentrates it on one person so everyone else does none.
Providers—OpenAI, Anthropic, OpenRouter—change nothing.
Administrators carry the full weight: obtaining keys, deploying beacons, naming
services, setting priorities. The zero-configuration experience exists because this person
configured everything in advance.
End users open an application, find AI available, use it. They never see Saturn’s
name. The closest analogy is AirPlay—join a network, open a compatible app, the
service is already there.
Developers write four lines: install, import, discover(), connect. No API key
management. No billing integration. No provider selection logic.

3.3

Concepts

The protocol rests on four abstractions.

3.3.1

Endpoints

Ollama, OpenRouter, LiteLLM, vLLM, and dozens of other providers already serve the
OpenAI-compatible REST API—JSON messages in, completions out. Saturn adopts
that interface wholesale. A Saturn endpoint is any HTTP service behind it: cloud
provider, university data center, single-board computer running quantized models. If

13

the service handles the paths in Section 3.4.3, it qualifies. No code changes, no Saturnspecific integration.

3.3.2

Beacons

Beacons do not touch API traffic. A beacon registers a service under saturn. tcp.local.,
publishes metadata in DNS TXT records, and answers discovery queries. Clients connect to the advertised endpoint directly—the beacon says where to go and how to authenticate but never sees a prompt or a response.
That separation has consequences. Kill the beacon and active connections survive;
clients already holding an endpoint address keep using it. The beacon’s only job is
DNS registration and credential management, which is why it runs on hardware as
constrained as a travel router (Section 4.4).
Two types exist. A cloud beacon announces a remote provider and hands out
ephemeral API keys (Section 3.3.4). A local beacon announces something on the
LAN—an Ollama instance, a vLLM server—and pulls the DNS entry when the service goes down.

3.3.3

Priorities

A network might run a local GPU and keep a cloud API as fallback. Priority—a number
in each service’s TXT record—determines which wins. Lower beats higher: priority 1
over priority 50. Semantics follow DNS SRV convention.
The administrator who wants local-first inference assigns priority 1 to the GPU,
priority 50 to the cloud. Users get whichever is available without choosing or knowing
the difference. Priority is policy, not performance—the administrator decides what
“best” means for this network.

14

3.3.4

Ephemeral Keys Broadcasting an API key to every device on the segment hands credentials to anyone
listening. Saturn limits the damage through time. Cloud beacons generate keys that live
ten minutes on the provider’s server and rotate every five. Old and new keys overlap
so clients never lose connectivity mid-request. Ten minutes after issuance, the beacon
revokes the old key through the provider’s API.
Record a credential from a broadcast, walk out of the building, try it from a coffee
shop—revoked. The mechanism resembles Kerberos ticket distribution: time-limited
credentials scoped to a session, applied here at the application layer through provider
key provisioning APIs.
Local deployments skip the mechanism entirely. An Ollama instance on a private
network needs no authentication; the TXT record’s features field (Section 3.4.2) says
so. Cloud deployments require that the provider support programmatic key creation and
deletion—OpenRouter and DeepInfra do; providers without provisioning APIs cannot
participate.

3.4

Protocol Specification

What follows, combined with RFCs 6762 and 6763, is enough to build a conforming
implementation from scratch.

3.4.1

Service Type

Saturn services register under the DNS-SD service type saturn. tcp.local.. The
underscore-prefixed format follows RFC 6763 naming conventions for DNS-SD service
types [8]. The tcp designator indicates that clients connect via TCP after discovery.

15

The .local. suffix confines discovery to the link-local multicast domain defined by
mDNS [9].

3.4.2

Record Structure

Saturn reuses the DNS-SD record chain from Section 2.2.2. Three record types cooperate per service instance. A PTR record maps saturn. tcp.local. to instance
names—the enumeration step. An SRV record per instance gives hostname and port.
A TXT record per instance carries the rest: deployment type, API compatibility, priority, and—for cloud beacons—an ephemeral credential. Once a client has all three
records, it can connect without further queries.
Table 3.1 specifies the TXT record fields that constitute the Saturn protocol.
Optional extensions exist for model enumeration (models), capability advertisement (capabilities), context window size (context), and pricing tier (cost). Clients
ignore fields they do not recognize. Every value is a UTF-8 string; every key-value pair
must fit the 255-byte DNS TXT string limit from Section 2.2.1.

3.4.3

Endpoint Expectations

Three HTTP paths are mandatory (Table 3.2).
Cloud beacons are different: api base points to the upstream provider, not to the
beacon. Clients extract the ephemeral key from the TXT record and authenticate with
the provider directly.

3.4.4

Discovery Process

Discovery is four steps:

16

Table 3.1: Saturn TXT Record Schema
Presence Description
Required Protocol version (currently 1.0).

ollama).
api base

http://192.168.1.5:11434/v1
for
local
services;
https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
for
cloud beacons).

beacons with key rotation; network proxy
for local services.

ephemeral key

TXT string limit.

rotation interval Beacon
alongside ephemeral key.
Field
version
deployment Step 1: Browse. The client multicasts a DNS-SD PTR query for saturn. tcp.local.,
requesting the names of all Saturn service instances on the local network.
Step 2: Resolve. For each discovered instance, the client queries the corresponding
SRV and TXT records. The SRV record yields a hostname and port; the TXT record
provides metadata including deployment type, API base URL, priority, and—for cloud
beacons—an ephemeral credential.
Step 3: Sort. The client orders discovered services by the priority field in ascending order. Ties may be broken by implementation-defined criteria such as the cost
field or random selection.
Step 4: Select. The client issues a GET /v1/health request to the highest-priority
service. If the service responds, the client connects. If not, the client proceeds to the
17

Table 3.2: Required Saturn Endpoint Paths
Method Path
Behavior
GET
/v1/health
Returns a JSON object with a status
field. Clients issue this request to verify service availability before and during use.
GET
/v1/models
Returns a JSON array of available
model identifiers in the OpenAI listmodels format.
POST
/v1/chat/completions Accepts a JSON body containing a
messages array and model identifier. Returns a chat completion response. Must support both streaming (server-sent events) and nonstreaming modes.
next service in priority order. Discovery fails only when no service responds.
If a connected service drops—failed health check or failed API request—the client
starts over from Step 1. 3.5

Architecture Decisions

A proxy would be simpler for clients—one URL, one connection—but the proxy sees
every prompt and response, fails and takes the whole network with it, and must carry
throughput it didn’t generate. Beacons sidestep all three. Clients discover endpoints at
startup and reconnect on failure; the beacon itself never touches API traffic.
Multicast confines discovery to a single broadcast segment. A centralized registry
would cross subnets and look things up faster. It would also need an operator, a domain
name, TLS certificates, and an availability plan. mDNS needs the network and nothing
else. The scope limitation is deliberate: Saturn targets home networks, campus WiFi,
small offices—places where one segment is the whole network.
The specification defines a service type, a TXT record schema, and endpoint con18

tracts. It does not prescribe a language, an mDNS library, or an SDK. Seven implementations in four languages (Section 5.1.3) prove the point: interoperability follows from
the wire format, not from shared code.
Image generation, audio synthesis, and embedding computation each use different
API shapes. Saturn handles text completion only—the capability the OpenAI API standardizes and the one deployed most widely. Other modalities would need their own
TXT schema extensions and endpoint contracts, not a redesign of the mDNS/DNS-SD
layer.

19

Implementation.score_string:
Chapter 4
Implementation
4.0.1

Server Architecture

The core Python package handles beacon registration and mDNS queries. On macOS
it talks to Bonjour; on Linux, Avahi. When neither daemon is available, it falls back
to raw UDP multicast on 224.0.0.251:5353. The beacon keeps its TXT records fresh,
re-registers before the mDNS TTL expires, and—for cloud deployments—rotates API
credentials on schedule.

4.0.2

Client Discovery

The client side mirrors the four-step process from Section 3.4.4. discover() browses
saturn. tcp.local., resolves SRV and TXT records, sorts by priority, and healthchecks each service in order. connect(service) pulls the ephemeral key from the
TXT record and authenticates. Results cache for 60 seconds; a failed health check
triggers a fresh browse.

20

4.1

Server Types

4.1.1

Ollama Server

Ollama listens on port 11434 by default. The Ollama beacon checks that port (or a
user-specified one), confirms the instance responds to /v1/health, and registers it
over mDNS. It pulls the model list from Ollama’s /api/tags endpoint and writes it
into the TXT record, so clients see exactly which models are loaded without a separate
query.

4.1.2

OpenRouter Server

OpenRouter exposes a key provisioning API that lets callers mint, expire, and delete
sub-keys. The beacon uses this to implement Saturn’s rotation scheme. It authenticates
with the administrator’s master key, mints a 10-minute sub-key, broadcasts that sub-key
in the TXT record, and replaces it every 5 minutes. The old key gets deleted after a brief
grace period. The administrator’s permanent credential never appears on the network.

4.1.3

Fallback Server

The fallback beacon returns canned responses from hardcoded models. It exists for
testing: a developer can validate Saturn discovery end-to-end without Ollama running
or cloud credentials configured.

21

4.2

Client Integration Patterns

4.2.1

Native SDK Integration

Cursor, Continue, and Open WebUI already accept arbitrary OpenAI-compatible endpoints. For these applications, Saturn integration means replacing the manual endpoint
field with a call to discover() and populating a dropdown from the result. Users pick
a service; no URLs, no credentials. The integration cost is one dependency and two or
three lines of configuration code.

4.2.2

Application Plugin

4.2.3

Tool Protocol Bridge

4.2.4

Subprocess Bridge

Applications that predate Saturn have no mDNS capability. A subprocess bridge works
around that: a small local proxy handles discovery on the application’s behalf.
VLC media player is the test case. Its users play video files; API keys mean nothing
to them. If Saturn can deliver AI inside VLC without any user configuration, the claim
extends beyond developer tooling.
User Experience
Installation: copy one directory into VLC’s extensions folder, restart. Activation: View
→ Extensions → Saturn Chat. No environment variables, no Python on the PATH, no
API key.
On activation, the extension reads metadata from whatever is playing—title, artist,
album, genre, timestamp—via VLC’s Lua metadata API. A dialog opens with two drop22

downs: discovered services and available models. The user types a question about the
media and gets a response. Discovery and model enumeration run in the background;
the user never sees them.
Technical Architecture
VLC’s extension system supports only Lua scripting with restricted HTTP capabilities
(GET requests only, no POST), and Lua has no native mDNS support. Two layers work
around these constraints.
Layer 1: A Python/FastAPI bridge service (‘vlc discovery bridge.py‘) runs as a
background subprocess launched by the Lua extension. The bridge performs mDNS
discovery using the same ‘dns-sd‘ subprocess calls as the core Saturn client, browses
‘ saturn. tcp.local.‘, resolves service addresses, maintains health checks, and exposes
an OpenAI-compatible REST API on localhost (127.0.0.1). When the Lua extension
activates, it launches the bridge with automatic port assignment. The bridge writes its
port to a file that the extension reads, establishing inter-process communication without
hardcoding ports.
Layer 2: The Lua extension (‘saturn chat.lua‘) creates a VLC dialog window, manages user interaction, formats requests as URL-encoded JSON payloads (working around
Lua’s GET-only limitation), and sends them to the localhost bridge via HTTP. When
the user deactivates the extension, the bridge is terminated.
The bridge is packaged into a standalone executable using PyInstaller, eliminating
the Python dependency for end users. Cross-platform backgrounding handles Windows
(‘start /B‘), macOS, and Linux process launching. The entire installation is a directory
copy; deployment requires no system-level configuration.

23

Significance
VLC is not a developer tool. If Saturn discovery works inside a media player whose
audience is non-technical, it works at the network infrastructure layer—not just in IDEs
and CLI scripts. The bridge code calls the same run dns sd discovery() function
as every other Saturn client: dns-sd -B saturn. tcp local followed by dns-sd
-L. Same protocol, same code path, different application. The subprocess pattern also
shows how to retrofit Saturn into software that Saturn’s developers do not control—any
application with a plugin or extension system can gain discovery through an identical
bridge.

4.3

GL.iNet Router Deployment

4.3.1

Hardware Constraints

The GL.iNet MT300N-V2 runs OpenWRT on 580 MB of RAM, 8 GB of flash, and
a single-core MIPS processor—far too weak for inference, but inference is not the
job. Registering mDNS records and rotating API keys costs kilobytes of memory and
milliseconds of CPU. The same router that already assigns IP addresses via DHCP and
resolves names via DNS now also advertises AI endpoints via Saturn.

4.3.2

OpenWRT Integration

Saturn ships as a custom OpenWRT package. The beacon starts on boot as a system
service, configured through UCI (Unified Configuration Interface)—the same tool administrators already use for DHCP pools and firewall rules. The administrator enters
cloud provider credentials or local service URLs; the beacon validates them at startup

24

and begins advertising. The package depends only on Python’s standard library, which
keeps the storage footprint small enough for the MT300N-V2’s 8 GB flash.

4.3.3

Significance

The router itself is incidental. What matters is that the device already configuring the
network—assigning IPs, resolving names—now also configures AI access. A parent
running a home network, or a small-office IT person managing a LAN, adds Saturn
to the same box they already maintain. Every device that joins the WiFi discovers AI
services alongside its IP address.

4.3.4

Limitations

The router does not scale to multi-site deployments. Two homes means two separate
configurations. If the router fails, discovery stops until it reboots. A multi-building
campus would need multiple routers or centralized infrastructure that Saturn currently
does not provide. These are edge-deployment constraints, not Saturn-specific failures.

25

tex_context: H1 & Visibility of system status & Servers appear on network via mDNS; logs show registration of addresses, ports, and service names \\
H5 & Error prevention & Auto-assigns host, port, priority, and failover; defaults to alternate services before erroring \\
H6 & Recognition over recall & \texttt{discover()} returns available services---no URLs, keys, or model strings to remember \\

Evaluation.score_string:
Chapter 5
Evaluation
5.1

Evaluation Approach

Either a working system that provisions AI via mDNS/DNS-SD without user configuration exists, or it does not. Throughput percentiles and scalability curves are beside
the point—they measure how well, not whether. Three requirements decompose the
claim.
R1: Service Discovery. Saturn services can be found on the local network without
endpoint URLs, API keys, or configuration files on the client side.
R2: Connection Sufficiency. The information carried in mDNS/DNS-SD records provides everything a client needs to establish a working API connection, with no
out-of-band configuration.
R3: Cross-Implementation Interoperability. Independent implementations in different languages consume the same protocol records without a bridging or adaptation layer.

26

R1 is straightforward: can a client with no prior knowledge find and connect?
R2 is more granular—every connection concern must map to a protocol field. R3
tests whether codebases built independently, in different languages, consume the same
records without adaptation.

5.1.1

R1: Service Discovery

[Hands-on demonstration pending.]

5.1.2

R2: Connection Sufficiency

Discovery alone solves nothing. The client still needs a network address, an API dialect,
credentials, and the right endpoint path. If any of those require a side channel—a
README, a .env file, a Slack message from IT—the system is not zero-configuration.
Siddiqui et al. [36] set the bar at “little end-user intervention”; connection sufficiency
asks whether that bar holds past discovery into actual use.
The resolution sequence follows RFC 6763 [8], extended with AI-specific metadata
in the TXT record:
1. Browse (PTR). The client multicasts a PTR query for saturn. tcp.local..
Each response names a Saturn service instance. Guttman [16] described this
mechanism as enabling “direct communications between two or more computing
devices via IP” without configuration.
2. Locate (SRV). The client queries each instance’s SRV record to get its hostname
and port.
3. Describe (TXT). The TXT record carries key-value pairs: api base (endpoint
URL for cloud deployments), api type (API compatibility identifier), ephemeral key

27

(authentication credential), auth (authentication method), priority (routing
preference), and deployment (cloud or network).
4. Resolve (A/AAAA). The client resolves the SRV target hostname to an IP address.
Table 5.1 maps each concern to its DNS record. No row requires out-of-band information.
Table 5.1: Connection concerns mapped to DNS-SD records.
Connection Concern Record
Field(s)
Out-of-Band?
Service existence
PTR
instance name
No
Host and port
SRV
target, port
No
IP address
A/AAAA address
No
No
API endpoint URL
TXT
api base
API compatibility
TXT
api type
No
No
Authentication
TXT
ephemeral key, auth
Routing priority
TXT
priority
No

The TXT record does not just describe a service—it hands the client a working credential. For cloud backends, the ephemeral key field carries a live API key that Saturn
generates and rotates on a five-minute schedule. Clients pull this key at discovery time
and pass it as a Bearer token. No account creation. No key management portal.
Three backends confirm the schema handles different authentication mechanisms
without modification (Table 5.2).
Table 5.2: API backends operating through Saturn’s TXT record schema.
Backend
api type Auth Mechanism Deployment Confirmed In
Ollama
ollama None (local)
Network
Core, Router, AI SDK
DeepInfra
openai Scoped JWT
Cloud beacon Core
OpenRouter openai Ephemeral key
Cloud beacon Core, Router
Ollama needs no authentication—auth reads none, ephemeral key is empty. DeepInfra uses scoped JWTs that expire automatically; providers/deepinfra.py gener28 ates them. OpenRouter uses provisioned API keys with explicit revocation; providers/openrouter.py
creates, rotates, and revokes them. The client code path is the same in all three cases:
read the TXT record, extract the key if present, authenticate, call the endpoint. Syed et
al. [40] documented the per-application credential burden that AI-as-a-Service imposes;
Saturn’s TXT schema collapses it into one network broadcast.
Limitation. The TXT record schema describes how to connect to a service but not
what the service can do at fine granularity. The optional capabilities field advertises
broad categories (chat, code, vision), but per-model attributes—context window size,
tool-use support, embedding dimensions—fall outside the discovery record. Clients
that need this information query the /v1/models endpoint after connecting. This reflects a protocol constraint: mDNS TXT records are limited to 255 bytes per key-value
pair [9], and per-model metadata would exceed that budget.

5.1.3

R3: Cross-Implementation Interoperability

If Saturn worked only in the reference Python package, it would be a library, not a
protocol. Hidden assumptions, hard-coded paths, implicit dependencies—impossible
to rule out from one codebase. Cross-implementation interoperability is the real test:
does the specification carry the design, or does the reference code?
Seven implementations consume saturn. tcp.local. records (Table 5.3).
Four mDNS libraries underpin these implementations (Table 5.4). Saturn patches
none of them—each implements RFC 6762 and RFC 6763 on its own and resolves
saturn. tcp.local. through its own protocol stack.
Four integration patterns. The core package, saturn-router, and ai-sdk-provider embed mDNS queries directly. Open WebUI hooks in through its plugin system. saturnmcp exposes discovery through Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol, so AI coding as29

Table 5.3: Saturn implementation matrix. Seven consumers span three languages, four
mDNS libraries, and four integration patterns.

sistants like Cursor find Saturn services through their existing tool interface. The VLC
extension takes the most roundabout path: VLC’s Lua engine cannot open multicast
sockets, so a FastAPI subprocess handles discovery and exposes an OpenAI-compatible
API on localhost.
mDNS started with printers [21], moved to IoT [37], and now reaches AI provisioning. The interoperability evidence is structural: four mDNS libraries, each implementing RFC 6762 on its own, resolve the same service type without modification.
Limitation. All seven implementations share a single author. Independent development teams would strengthen the interoperability claim. However, the four mDNS
libraries—zeroconf, mdns-sd, multicast-dns, and Apple’s dns-sd—are maintained
by independent developers with no knowledge of Saturn. The protocol’s interoperability rests on these libraries’ implementations of mDNS/DNS-SD, not on shared code
between Saturn consumers.

30

5.2

Configuration Effort (C2)

Saturn pushes AI configuration onto one person. Developers and end users do nothing.
Benefits compound with headcount—ten users means ten fewer credential setups. Cognitive walkthroughs [41] measure it: count steps across three personas against a shared
completion criterion.

5.2.1

Methodology A cognitive walkthrough [41] traces a user through an interface step by step, checking
whether the correct action is identifiable at each point. We adapted it to compare Saturn
against traditional API-key provisioning, starting from infrastructure setup and ending
at a working AI feature in an end user’s hands.
The completion criterion: an end user on the network uses an AI-powered feature in
an application. Both paradigms must reach this finish line. Without a shared endpoint,
Saturn’s full pipeline gets measured against the traditional paradigm’s first step—unfair.
Three personas span the chain:
1. Sysadmin/IT — provisions AI access at the infrastructure level.
2. App developer — builds an application that consumes AI services.
3. End user — uses an app with AI features.
Both paradigms share eight prerequisite steps: sign up with OpenRouter, generate
a key, save it to .env. They diverge after that. Every walkthrough is an executable
Python file in the project repository; every import, function call, and configuration line
counts as one step.

31

5.2.2

Results

Sysadmin Configuration

Table 5.5: Sysadmin Configuration Steps

Traditionally, the sysadmin emails the key around, communicates the endpoint
URL, and repeats key creation per project—four steps past the prerequisites. Saturn
asks for more: install saturn-ai, configure a ServiceConfig object, call run service().
Six steps with the package, eighteen from scratch.
The Saturn sysadmin does 17% more work (or 117% more without the package).
What changes is the output. A traditional sysadmin hands off a raw key that every developer configures alone. A Saturn sysadmin leaves a running service on the network—
discoverable by any device, no further setup.

Six AI integration steps in the traditional paradigm: install the SDK, import modules, receive a key from IT, store it in an environment variable, instantiate a client,
make a test call. Then thirteen billing steps—Stripe account, pricing tiers, Stripe SDK,
checkout and webhook endpoints, subscription gating, payment testing. The billing
stack exists because the developer passes API costs to end users.
32

With Saturn: install saturn-ai and the OpenAI SDK, import discovery, call discover(),
point a client at whatever comes back. Four steps. The Stripe integration vanishes entirely because AI costs stay with the sysadmin.

Download, account creation, login—shared. The difference is payment. A traditional end user hits a paywall: navigate pricing, compare tiers, enter a credit card,
accept terms, confirm, return to the AI feature. Seven steps. Saturn has no paywall
because the developer never built billing. AI features work on first launch.

5.2.3

Analysis

Table 5.8: Configuration Steps by Persona (Saturn Package vs. Traditional)

The sysadmin gains two steps. Each developer loses fifteen. Each end user loses
seven. At 1+1+1 scale, total drops 53%.
The arithmetic scales. Traditional total effort is 12 + 19N + 7M for N developers
and M end users; Saturn’s is 14 + 4N + 0M. A department with 10 developers and 100
users: 12 + 190 + 700 = 902 steps the old way, 14 + 40 + 0 = 54 with Saturn. That is a
94% reduction.
33 Syed et al. [40] describe the baseline Saturn displaces: API keys, per-user accounts,
platform lock-in. Meli et al. [24] found that secret leakage scales with the number of
developers handling keys—Saturn takes developers out of that loop. Costa et al. [11]
treat usability as a dimension of AI democratization; Qazi [34] identifies payment friction specifically. Saturn drops the end-user step count from seven to zero.
Limitations. These walkthroughs were authored and counted by a single researcher.
The step counts are analytical—derived from executable walkthrough scripts—rather
than empirical measurements of time or error rates with human participants. A multievaluator study with timed task completion would strengthen external validity. The
walkthrough scripts are included in the project repository for reproducibility.

5.3

Usability Heuristic Evaluation

Step counts say nothing about interface quality. Nielsen’s ten usability heuristics [26]—
derived from factor analysis of 249 usability problems—fill that gap. Applying them
alongside the walkthrough gives two HCI lenses on the same system.

5.3.1

Method

All ten heuristics evaluated against Saturn’s three personas—sysadmin, developer, end
user—checking each against the interfaces that persona encounters. Results fall into
three bins: where Saturn helps, where it introduces friction, and where it fails outright.

5.3.2

Strengths

Four heuristics favor Saturn.

34

Table 5.9: Usability heuristics where Saturn demonstrates strength.
Heuristic Principle
Saturn Evidence
H1
Visibility of system status Servers appear on network via mDNS;
logs show registration of addresses, ports,
and service names
H5
Error prevention
Auto-assigns host, port, priority, and
failover; defaults to alternate services before erroring
H6
Recognition over recall
discover() returns available services—
no URLs, keys, or model strings to remember
H7
Flexibility and efficiency Python package for programmatic control;
traditional API key bypass remains available
Why 19 steps dropped to 4: discover() replaces recall-dependent tasks—typing
URLs, finding keys, setting environment variables—with a single function that shows
what the network offers. Nielsen [26] called this recognition over recall (H6). The
network itself is the retrieval cue.
Nobody types a URL, so nobody mistypes one. Auto-assigned hosts, ports, priorities, and failover targets remove the entire class of errors Nielsen [26] called “slips”—
unconscious mistakes from inattention (H5).

5.3.3

Trade-offs

Three heuristics cut both ways—Saturn’s choices help one persona at the expense of
another.
H4 (consistency) qualifies R3. Seven consumers interoperate on saturn. tcp.local.,
but interoperation at the service-type level does not mean schema consistency. Four
core fields—version, deployment, api type, priority—appear everywhere. Past
those, the schema drifts. TypeScript includes an auth field that Python and Rust lack.
Rust omits capabilities, cost, and context. Discovery works; the developer expe35

Table 5.10: Usability heuristics where Saturn presents trade-offs.
Heuristic Principle
Saturn Finding
H2
Match real-world conventions “Beacon” and “proxy” confuse sysadmins; developers and end users less affected
H3
User control and freedom
Developers pick servers by name; sysadmins stop services with ctrl-c; end users
have no service-level control
H4
Consistency and standards
TXT schema drifts across implementations:
auth field TypeScript-only;
capabilities, cost, context missing
from Rust
rience in mixed deployments degrades.
H2 (real-world match) hits the sysadmin hardest. “Beacon” describes Saturn’s architecture accurately, but a sysadmin hears it and thinks wireless access point. “Proxy”
in the codebase means API relay—not the HTTP/SOCKS proxy a sysadmin expects.
Developers mostly interact with discover() and never see either term in anger. End
users never see them at all.

5.3.4

Limitations Three heuristics expose gaps.
Table 5.11: Usability heuristics where Saturn shows limitations.
Heuristic Principle
Saturn Gap
H8
Minimalist design
TXT schema inconsistency adds noise;
saturn. tcp.local.
is specialistfacing
H9
Error recovery
Only “service not available” and “check
network”—no diagnostic guidance
H10
Help and documentation Docs cover sysadmin and developer audiences; end-user documentation is thin
H9 (error recovery) is the weakest point. discover() returns nothing and says

36

“service not available—check the network.” Server down? Multicast blocked? Firewall
eating port 5353? The message gives no clue. Nielsen [26] specified that error messages
should name the problem and suggest a fix. Saturn’s messages do neither. A diagnostic
mode that checks multicast reachability, lists nearby services, and suggests targeted
fixes would close this gap.
H8 (minimalist design) compounds H4. Mixed deployments show different TXT
schemas depending on which implementation advertised each service—noise where
minimalism demands consistency. The saturn. tcp.local. service type reads as
opaque to anyone not debugging mDNS traffic, though only sysadmins ever see it.

5.3.5

Synthesis

H6 and H5 account for the 79% developer step reduction: recognition replaces recall,
auto-configuration removes slips. The costs fall on the sysadmin—confusing terminology (H2), drifting schema (H4). Error recovery (H9) is the open wound: no selfdiagnosis at all.
Steps counted by the walkthrough, quality judged by the heuristics. Both are singleevaluator inspections; empirical user studies would strengthen external validity.

5.4

Security Analysis (C3)

mDNS broadcasts leak metadata. Traditional API key distribution happens out-of-band
and leaks nothing to the network. Saturn trades that privacy for convenience, and the
analysis below characterizes the cost. What the network exposes is determined by the
TXT record schema (Table A.1), not runtime behavior, so the analysis is protocol-level.
We compare Saturn against static API keys and OAuth using STRIDE, model exposure
windows, and assess the network constraints that shape real deployments.
37

5.4.1

Information Leakage Surface

Eleven metadata fields go out in every Saturn beacon’s TXT records (Table 5.12). The
protocol specification (Appendix A) defines all of them.
Table 5.12: Fields Exposed by Saturn Protocol Design
TXT Record Field Content
api base
Endpoint URL (e.g., http://host:8001/v1)
api type
API compatibility type (e.g., openai)
deployment
Deployment context (local or cloud)
priority
Routing priority value
version
Protocol version
capabilities
Supported modalities (e.g., chat)
context
Context window size
cost
Cost indicator
features
Optional feature flags
models
Available model identifiers
PTR record for service enumeration
service type
11 fields exposed. 0 credential material in base configuration.
Someone sitting on a Saturn network learns: which AI services exist, where they
run, which models they offer, and how they rank. Credentials are absent from local
deployments. Cloud deployments add an ephemeral key—ten-minute lifetime, fiveminute rotation. Prompts and responses stay invisible: beacons announce service locations, but API traffic flows client-to-endpoint over HTTPS.

5.4.2

STRIDE Threat Comparison

Table 5.13 lines up the three approaches across STRIDE’s six categories.
Static keys have three high-severity threats. Spoofing: a bearer token equates possession with identity. Repudiation: shared keys give no per-user attribution. Elevation
of privilege: steal an admin-scoped key, get full API access. Saturn eliminates all three
highs. mDNS announcements can be spoofed on the local segment, but ten-minute key
38 Table 5.13: STRIDE Severity by Credential Approach
Threat Category
Static Keys OAuth/SSO Saturn
Spoofing
High
Low
Medium
Tampering
Low
Low
Medium
Repudiation

Information Disclosure

Low

Elevation of Privilege

3/0/3

0/3/3
expiry caps the window—medium. Information disclosure is the starkest contrast: Meli
et al. [24] found 81% of leaked static keys on GitHub never revoked. Saturn keys expire
regardless.
The trade-off is geographic. Saturn’s three medium threats—spoofing, unsigned
records, multicast flooding—require physical LAN access. Static keys are exploitable
from anywhere on the internet.

5.4.3

Exposure Window Analysis

How long does a stolen credential remain dangerous? Table 5.14 answers.
Table 5.14: Credential Exposure Window Comparison
Metric
Static Key OAuth Token
Saturn Key
Max lifetime
Unbounded
1–24 hours
10 minutes
Rotation
Manual
Refresh flow Automatic (5 min)
Network scope
Global
Global
Local segment
Revocation
Manual (rarely done)
Automatic
Automatic
The dollar cost makes the difference concrete. At 10 requests/second and $0.01/request, a captured Saturn key maxes out at $60—ten minutes of traffic before the credential dies. A static key left undiscovered for one day: $8,640. Meli et al. [24] found
81% of leaked keys never revoked, so the static-key scenario has no ceiling.

39

5.4.4

AP Isolation and Network Constraints

Saturn assumes multicast reachability. Enterprise WiFi routinely breaks that assumption.
We tested in January 2026. Eduroam and UCSC-guest both block mDNS—clients
multicast to 224.0.0.251:5353 and hear nothing. The campus scenario from Chapter 1 fails on campus. Universities, libraries, shared spaces: the places where Saturn
says access matters most run AP isolation.
No protocol-level fix exists. The decision to block multicast belongs to campus IT;
Saturn cannot route around it without network changes—enabling multicast forwarding
or deploying on a dedicated segment. VLANs without multicast bridging and firewalls
blocking UDP 5353 create the same failure.
Saturn works where the administrator controls the network and multicast flows
freely: home WiFi, small offices, lab networks, wired Ethernet. It fails where client
isolation blocks multicast—an mDNS limitation (RFC 6762 [9]), not a Saturn-specific
one.

40

Appendix A.score_string:
Appendix A
Saturn Protocol Specification
This appendix specifies the Saturn protocol as a standalone reference. An implementor
reading only this appendix and the RFCs it cites should produce a system interoperable
with any conforming Saturn deployment.

A.1

Service Registration

A Saturn beacon registers one DNS-SD service instance under the type saturn. tcp.local.
(RFC 6763 [8]). Registration creates three DNS records:
1. A PTR record mapping saturn. tcp.local. to the instance name (e.g.,
ollama-lab. saturn. tcp.local.).
2. An SRV record providing the beacon’s hostname and port.
3. A TXT record carrying service metadata as key-value pairs.
The instance name is a human-readable UTF-8 string chosen by the administrator.
It must be unique within the multicast domain. The .local. suffix confines all queries
and responses to the link-local scope defined by mDNS (RFC 6762 [9]).

A.2

TXT Record Schema

Table A.1 defines all TXT record fields. One DNS TXT string encodes one key-value
pair in key=value format and must fit within 255 bytes (RFC 6763, Section 6.1).
Clients ignore unrecognized keys.

A.3

Endpoint Requirements

Every URL advertised in api base must implement three HTTP paths:
GET /v1/health Returns JSON with a status field. Clients call this to verify liveness before use and periodically during sessions. A non-200 response or timeout
(recommended: 5 seconds) indicates the service is unavailable.
GET /v1/models Returns a JSON object with a data array of model objects, each
containing at minimum an id field. Follows the OpenAI list-models response
format.
POST /v1/chat/completions Accepts a JSON body with model (string) and messages
(array of {role, content} objects). Returns a chat completion response. Must
support both non-streaming (JSON response) and streaming (server-sent events
with data: prefix) modes, selected by the stream boolean in the request body.
For cloud beacons, api base points to the upstream provider, not the beacon.
Clients authenticate with the provider using the ephemeral key from the TXT record.

A.4

Discovery Algorithm

Listing A.4 specifies client discovery in pseudocode.

function discover():
instances = mdns_browse("_saturn._tcp.local.")
services = []
for instance in instances:
srv = mdns_resolve_srv(instance)
txt = mdns_resolve_txt(instance)
if txt["version"].major != 1:
skip

// incompatible protocol version

services.append({
host: srv.hostname,
port: srv.port,
metadata: txt
})
sort services by metadata["priority"] ascending
for service in services:
url = service.metadata["api_base"]
headers = {}
if "ephemeral_auth" in service.metadata["features"]:
headers["Authorization"] =
"Bearer " + service.metadata["ephemeral_key"]
response = http_get(url + "/v1/health",
headers, timeout=5s)
if response.status == 200:
return service
return null

// connected

// no healthy service found

Clients may cache discovery results. A recommended cache TTL is 60 seconds;
clients should re-browse when the cached service fails a health check.

A.5

Ephemeral Key Rotation Protocol

Cloud beacons rotate API credentials on a fixed schedule to bound exposure windows.
The rotation proceeds as follows:
1. At startup, the beacon creates a new API key via the provider’s provisioning API
and records its creation time t0 .
2. The beacon publishes the key in the ephemeral key TXT field and sets rotation interval
to the rotation period R (default: 300 seconds).
3. At t0 + R, the beacon creates a new key k1 , updates the TXT record to advertise
k1 , and starts a grace timer for the old key k0 .
4. After a grace period G (default: 5 seconds), the beacon deletes k0 from the
provider’s system.
5. Steps 3–4 repeat every R seconds.
6. On shutdown, the beacon deletes the current key from the provider.
The key lifetime L (default: 600 seconds) exceeds the rotation interval R, so at most
two keys are valid simultaneously during the overlap window. This guarantees zerodowntime transitions: clients using the previous key continue working while the TXT
record advertises its replacement.

A.6

Conformance

A conforming Saturn beacon must:
• Register exactly one saturn. tcp.local. service instance per advertised endpoint.
• Include all six required TXT fields (Table A.1).
• Include ephemeral key and rotation interval when features contains ephemeral auth.
• Deregister the mDNS service on shutdown.
• Delete active ephemeral keys from the provider on shutdown.
A conforming Saturn client must:
• Discover services via mDNS PTR query for saturn. tcp.local..
• Parse all required TXT fields; ignore unrecognized fields.
• Sort discovered services by priority ascending.
• Verify service health via GET /v1/health before use.
• Fall back to the next-priority service when the current service becomes unreachable.

Table A.1: Complete TXT Record Field Reference

server).

Appendix B.score_string:
Appendix B
Generative AI in Thesis Development
B.1

Knowledge Graph Authorship

Saturn’s argument draws on twenty-odd papers and six codebases, with evidence for
each claim bleeding into the others. By week three a flat document could not hold
it. We switched to a JSON knowledge graph we call moons—nodes for concepts, papers, claims, components; edges typed as supports, contrasts, or demonstrated by. Five
hundred lines of structure; the real content lives in linked markdown files.
Claude built edges faster than we could. Before generating any, we nailed down
semantics: “supports” means the paper provides direct evidence for our claim; “related”
means a connection exists but the paper does not argue our point. Getting that right
mattered more than speed. Claude then proposed roughly a hundred candidate edges.
Siddiqui 2012 got tagged as supporting claim-1, which is defensible—but only if you
skip the abstract and read the section on heterogeneous sensor networks. That kind of
half-truth was typical. We verified every edge against the actual source text.
The cross-domain links justified the tool. We would not have connected Kaiser
and Waldvogel’s mDNS privacy analysis to Bassignana’s AI accessibility data on our

49

own—Claude surfaced that in seconds. We still rewrote most auto-generated edge descriptions, caught contradictions Claude missed, and dropped edges that crumbled on
re-reading.

B.2

Iterative Advisory Role

Starting January 2026, we used Claude as a sounding board: describe a draft section,
get pushback, decide what survives. Gaps surfaced that we had not seen.
Claim-2 originally read “reducing configuration effort.” Claude’s first question:
compared to what? We had no baseline. That forced us to define the status quo—
developers managing cloud API keys per-app with no local fallback—and redesign the
evaluation to measure against it. A different exchange caught a contradiction between
claim-1 and claim-3. If mDNS lets anyone broadcast a service, then an attacker can
broadcast a fake one. We had not separated what Saturn guarantees at the infrastructure
level (nothing—mDNS is unauthenticated by design) from what it mitigates at the application layer (ephemeral keys, priority-based selection). Sorting that out rewired the
threat model.
Claude suggested framing the introduction around a forced choice—manual setup
or expensive cloud APIs—rather than the weaker “manual configuration is error-prone.”
We used a version of that framing.
Not every suggestion survived. A proposed fourth claim about cost savings would
have diluted the access-equity argument. Monte Carlo simulation for scalability predictions depended on parameter estimates we could not validate; we ran real trials on a
physical router instead. Quantum computing threats got scoped out in favor of classical
attack vectors. A DRM suggestion for model access control was wrong for Saturn’s
audience, but it forced us to articulate why Saturn deliberately omits access control—a 50

gap in the draft we had not noticed.

B.3

Limits and Human Judgment

The thesis would exist without Claude. The graph would have taken longer; some crossdomain connections—linking mDNS privacy research to AI accessibility data—would
have required more deliberate searching; the contradiction between claim-1 and claim3 might have survived another draft or two. Design decisions, claim structure, evidence
judgments, and all code are ours. Claude was useful for speed and for catching things
we had stared past.
We disclose all of this because Saturn argues AI should be accessible. Using it
while hiding it would contradict the project’s own thesis.

51
